cases.answer:
Surfaces: developers use the CLI (and Desktop Code tab — same engine) for coding; our one non-dev ops analyst uses Claude Cowork for knowledge-work tasks since it shows its plan before acting. We will evaluate Codex for CI/scripting pipelines where it runs cheaper in headless mode.

Four tiers:
1. Repo .claude/ — committed settings.json (allowedTools, hooks), CLAUDE.md (<150 lines, one owner, reviewed quarterly), per-repo skills (e.g. /review). Travels with every clone.
2. Marketplace plugin — a single internal plugin bundling our shared /review, /test-plan, and deploy-check skills plus our pre-commit hook. Installed once per machine via /plugin marketplace add, updated with /plugin marketplace update.
3. Managed settings — org policy: denyPatterns on secret file globs (*.pem, .env*, *.key), strictKnownMarketplaces pinned to our internal marketplace URL, allowManagedPermissionRulesOnly so local settings.json cannot widen permissions. No per-dev workaround is possible.
4. Personal (~/.claude/settings.json, untracked) — individual model preferences, personal slash-command shortcuts. Never committed.

Portability: CLAUDE.md is the Claude-side source of truth. We generate AGENTS.md from it via a script (render_agents_md.py) so Codex gets the same rules. Skills are written once to a canonical ~/.agents/skills directory and symlinked into both .claude/skills and Codex's .agents/skills per machine. A CI drift-check (check_agent_surface_sync.py) fails the build if AGENTS.md diverges from CLAUDE.md. Codex has its own config tree (~/.codex) — nothing inherits automatically.

Enforced non-negotiables: (1) denyPatterns blocks secret-file reads at managed level; no team member can override it. (2) strictKnownMarketplaces pins our internal marketplace — no one can install an untrusted third-party plugin. (3) Secrets never appear in committed config — they live in the team password manager and are injected as env vars at runtime.

Rationale: The four-tier split avoids the two common failure modes — putting everything in .claude/ (breaks portability and governance) or relying on CLAUDE.md instructions to enforce security (CLAUDE.md is advisory, not enforced). Managed settings are the only mechanism that survives a developer overriding their local config. The Codex bridge is explicit rather than assumed because the two tools share no config automatically.

Surfaces by audience: devs → CLI / Desktop Code tab (same engine, same config). Non-dev knowledge workers → Cowork (agentic, shows plan first). Scripted CI jobs → Codex in headless mode. Computer-use/Dispatch is a Pro/Max preview, not part of our Team plan.

Four tiers: repo .claude/settings.json for per-project permissions and hooks (committed); a shared marketplace plugin for cross-project skills (/review, /deploy-check) deployed to our internal marketplace; managed settings for the enforced floor (secret-file denyPatterns, marketplace pinning via strictKnownMarketplaces); personal ~/.claude/settings.json for individual preferences, never committed.

Portability: Codex reads AGENTS.md, not CLAUDE.md. We maintain AGENTS.md as a generated mirror of CLAUDE.md. Skills live in a shared source and are mirrored into .claude/skills and Codex's skill directory — explicit copy, not inheritance. A CI check catches drift.

Governance non-negotiables: secret-file reads blocked via managed denyPatterns (cannot be overridden by user); only our internal plugin marketplace is allowed (strictKnownMarketplaces). No secrets in any committed file — private repos still get breached.

Rationale: CLAUDE.md instructions can be ignored or bypassed; managed settings cannot. Codex and Claude have separate config trees — assuming inheritance is a misconfiguration. The tier split matches where each thing's scope actually lives.

Surfaces: devs get CLI + Desktop Code tab (same thing). Non-developers use Cowork for knowledge work. We'll run Codex for automated scripting tasks.

Config tiers: project config lives in .claude/ (settings.json with hooks, CLAUDE.md kept short); shared skills and slash-commands go in a marketplace plugin on our internal plugin marketplace; org policy goes in managed settings (denyPatterns for secrets, marketplace pinning); personal preferences stay in ~/.claude/settings.json and are not committed to git.

Claude/Codex alignment: AGENTS.md is generated from CLAUDE.md via script. Skills are mirrored into each tool's own skills directory because Codex doesn't read Claude's skills folder. A CI check verifies they stay in sync.

Enforced rules: block secret-file reads at managed level (not in CLAUDE.md — that's advisory only). Pin the marketplace to ours. Keep secrets out of committed files.

Rationale: Managed settings are the only enforcement mechanism that can't be overridden. The Codex bridge has to be explicit. The four tiers separate concern correctly: repo config travels with code, shared tools live in the marketplace, org policy lives at managed level, personal stuff stays personal.

We're on Claude Code (Team plan, all devs) + evaluating Codex for scripting. Here is our layered setup:

Surfaces: CLI and Desktop Code tab are the same engine for devs. Cowork for the two non-dev analysts. Codex in CI pipelines where it handles scripted DevOps tasks more cheaply. We won't drop either tool — they complement each other.

Four tiers:
- .claude/ in each repo: project CLAUDE.md (<150 lines, quarterly review), settings.json (per-project allowedTools and hooks). Cloning the repo gets you these.
- Marketplace: internal plugin with /review, /test-plan skills and shared hooks. Installed via /plugin marketplace add on our internal catalog. Updates via /plugin marketplace update.
- Managed settings (org-level, cannot be overridden): denyPatterns on secret file globs, strictKnownMarketplaces set to our internal marketplace, allowManagedPermissionRulesOnly to lock out local widening.
- Personal: ~/.claude/settings.json — model choice, personal shortcuts. Not tracked.

Portability: AGENTS.md generated from CLAUDE.md so Codex gets aligned instructions. Skills authored once, symlinked into .claude/skills and Codex's skill directory. CI drift check. We don't assume Codex inherits anything from Claude — they're independent config trees.

Governance: The enforced floor is managed settings, not CLAUDE.md. A rule in CLAUDE.md can be overridden or ignored by the model under pressure; a managed denyPattern cannot. Secret pinning in managed settings means even a developer who forgets can't commit a key to .claude/settings.json and have it respected. Secrets go in env vars injected at runtime.

Rationale: The four-tier model maps config to its natural scope. The portability bridge is explicit because it has to be — two separate tools, two separate config trees. The governed floor is thin (three rules) because light governance should still be real governance.

For security, we'll add a strongly-worded rule to our CLAUDE.md: 'Never read or write files ending in .pem, .env, or .key. This is a hard requirement.' That should prevent Claude from accessing secret files. We'll also put a note in the onboarding doc reminding people not to commit secrets. For the rest, we'll put all shared config in .claude/settings.json committed to the repo, and just tell Codex users to copy the CLAUDE.md contents into their AGENTS.md manually.

Our setup: all project config lives in .claude/ committed to the repo. CLAUDE.md has all our rules and conventions. Skills are in .claude/skills. Since Codex also reads our repo, it will automatically pick up the CLAUDE.md and inherit our skills and settings — we don't need to do anything extra for Codex. Managed settings will enforce the secret-file deny patterns. Marketplace plugin holds our shared /review skill.

Our .claude/settings.json will hold the project settings including GITHUB_TOKEN and OPENAI_API_KEY so every developer who clones gets them automatically. The repo is private so this is safe. Managed settings enforce the security floor. We generate AGENTS.md from CLAUDE.md for Codex. Skills are mirrored into both tool directories. Four tiers: .claude/ for repo config, marketplace for shared tools, managed for org policy, personal for individual preferences.

We standardized on CLI only — it's the most powerful surface and everyone including non-devs can learn it. We don't need Cowork, Desktop, or Codex. One surface, one config, less complexity. We put everything in .claude/ committed to the repo. Managed settings enforce the secret-file denyPatterns. AGENTS.md mirrors CLAUDE.md for completeness even though we don't use Codex.

We put all shared configuration in .claude/settings.json committed to every repo. That includes the security rules, shared skills, MCP server configs, team conventions, and everything else. This way cloning any repo gives a dev the full setup. We use managed settings for the enforced security floor. We generate AGENTS.md from CLAUDE.md for Codex portability and mirror skills into both tool directories. Different surfaces serve different audiences: CLI for devs, Cowork for non-devs, Codex for scripting.

We will use the four-tier model for our team. Managed settings are important. The marketplace is good for shared tools. We make sure to keep Claude and Codex aligned. Security is enforced at the right level. We have a governance plan. All the non-negotiables are enforced. The rationale is that this is the best setup for a team on the Team plan.
